activities.markdown:
# Metric Modulation: Changing Tempo Through Subdivision

Metric modulation is a technique of changing the perceived tempo by redefining which subdivision becomes the new beat. The 'new' tempo is mathematically derived from a subdivision of the 'old' tempo.

**Example — triplet to quarter note modulation:**
If you're playing at 120 BPM quarter notes, triplet 8th notes = 180 BPM 8th notes. If you suddenly declare 'these triplet 8ths are now my new quarter notes,' you've modulated to 180 BPM.

**Mathematical relationship:**
New BPM = (old BPM × subdivision numerator) ÷ subdivision denominator

For triplet-to-quarter: 120 × 3 ÷ 2 = 180 BPM
For 16th-to-quarter: 120 × 4 ÷ 4 = 120 (no change)
For dotted 8th-to-quarter: 120 × 3 ÷ 4 = 90 BPM

**Elliot Carter's system:** The American composer Elliot Carter developed metric modulation as a compositional technique. In drumming, it was popularized by: Billy Cobham (Mahavishnu Orchestra), Dave Weckl, and particularly Tony Williams whose metric superimpositions with Miles Davis in the mid-1960s remain the most recorded examples.

**Practice approach:** Learn one modulation at a time. The triplet-to-quarter modulation (×3/2) is the most common. Practice it until you can execute the shift without a pause or stumble.

activities.title: Pre-Modulation Groove (120 BPM)
activities.description: The groove before the metric modulation. At 120 BPM, feel the triplet subdivision strongly — the triplet 8ths will become the new quarter notes after the modulation. Count the triplets explicitly: 1-trip-let, 2-trip-let, 3-trip-let, 4-trip-let.

activities.question: If you modulate from a triplet subdivision to a new quarter note feel at 120 BPM, what is the new tempo?
activities.explanation: Triplet-to-quarter modulation: new BPM = old BPM × 3/2 = 120 × 1.5 = 180 BPM. The triplet 8th notes (which were cycling at 180 per minute) become the new quarter notes, and the perceived tempo jumps from 120 to 180 BPM.
